data.title: Uber to buy cab-ordering platform FlyTaxi ahead of ride-hailing regulation
data.description:
Uber has announced it is acquiring the Hong Kong-based online cab-hailing platform FlyTaxi to expand its taxi services, ahead of the implementation of a regulatory regime for ride-hailing services later this year.
In a statement on Friday, Uber said that its acquisition of FlyTaxi was part of its commitment to help grow the taxi industry and provide “greater economic opportunities” for drivers.
It also said that FlyTaxi would continue to operate as normal and that nothing would change for drivers and riders using either the app for Uber or the home-grown taxi-hailing service.
“Uber has been investing in Hong Kong’s taxi industry for well over a decade, and this acquisition partnership reinforces our deep commitment to the city,” said Estyn Chung, general manager of Uber Hong Kong.
“By combining FlyTaxi’s local expertise with Uber’s technology, we are empowering drivers to grow their businesses and continuing to give riders the exceptional experience they expect.”
FlyTaxi is a mobile taxi app that connects riders and drivers. Founded by Simon Siu, it served as one of the first online ride-hailing platforms in Hong Kong when it launched in 2013, and remains widely used across the city.
“Joining forces with Uber is a proud milestone for our company, allowing us to leverage global technology to ensure the local taxi industry continues to innovate and thrive,” Siu said of the acquisition.

Uber previously bought another of the city’s most popular cab-hailing apps, HKTaxi, in 2021. The service was shut down last year and migrated its drivers to the Uber app.
Uber launched metered taxi services through its platform in partnership with the now-closed app in 2023.
The move comes as the city prepares for ride-hailing platforms to formally launch under a new licensing regime in the fourth quarter of this year.
Currently, it is illegal in Hong Kong for private vehicle drivers to accept paid customers without a hire-car permit, but ride-hailing platforms such as Uber, Tada, Amap and Didi Chuxing operate such services without regulation.
Amap is operated by Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
The licensing system sets requirements for vehicle age and insurance, while both ride-hailing platforms, drivers and the vehicles will be required to have separate licences and permits.
The taxi industry’s years-long frustration with competition from ride-hailing platforms reached a fever pitch in recent years, to the point where Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu had to tell cab drivers to stop launching sting operations against Uber drivers in 2024.
Francis Fong Po-kiu, honorary president of the Hong Kong Information Technology Federation, described Uber’s acquisition of FlyTaxi as a way for the ride-hailing giant to “reduce competition and further solidify its taxi booking service in Hong Kong”.
“This acquisition also aims to mitigate the impact of uncertainty surrounding the launch of its ride-hailing service in Hong Kong in the fourth quarter,” he said.

data.title: No-drone zone: DJI, consumers grapple with Beijing sales ban
data.description:
A sweeping rule change for drones in Beijing, which bars sales in the Chinese capital and requires identity registration linked to each device from Friday, has left sellers and consumers scrambling to cope with some of the country’s toughest regulations on the sector.
At a flagship retail store operated by DJI, the world’s largest drone maker, in Beijing’s bustling Guomao area, a shop assistant said that the store had been preparing to clear its shelves of all drone products in accordance with the ban.
The store was set to remove all drone products – from the affordable Neo series to the most expensive Mavic models – on Thursday, the employee said, adding that the shop was running low on stock due to an influx of customers snapping products before it was too late.
Beijing’s legislature approved the new regulation on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), a category that includes drones, in late March. In a first for the country, it prohibits the transport of drones into the city as well as their sale.
E-commerce platforms, including Alibaba Group Holding’s Taobao marketplace and rival JD.com, will also halt sales and shipments of drones to Beijing from Friday.
Some DJI stores have been dealing with crowds of customers trying to take stock of the new regulation’s impact, deciding whether to buy backup devices or upgrade before the sales ban.
At least four DJI official stores in Beijing have been contacting customers to inform them of the new rule and advising them to make necessary purchases or upgrades while they still can, according to a screenshot of messages sent to potential clients and seen by the South China Morning Post.
DJI did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the new rule’s effect on its business in Beijing.


Meanwhile, drone fans in Beijing were rushing to comply with the requirement to register their newly bought and existing drones, according to social media posts and DJI drone owners who spoke to the SCMP but requested anonymity speaking about a sensitive matter.
As of Friday, drone owners in Beijing are required to link their identity and contact information with the serial number of the device they own on the Unified Operation and Management (UOM) Platform managed by the Civil Aviation Administration of China. Owners can only keep registered devices, which they also must register with local police.
One avid drone fan in Beijing’s Changping suburb, who owns several DJI drones, said that he now only used the devices when travelling outside the city, because much of Beijing’s airspace has long been a no-fly zone.
Xiong Jinghua, a standing committee member of the Beijing municipal People’s Congress, said at the unveiling of the new rule in late March that Beijing, as the national capital, faced multiple challenges to its low-altitude safety, justifying the “urgent need” to strengthen control of UAVs.
Between August and October last year, Beijing authorities said they handled 51 cases of unauthorised drone flights, some of which posed risks to commercial aviation, according to a report by state-backed Xinhua News Agency last year.
A user who spent more than 13,000 yuan (US$1,901) on a DJI Mavic 3 Pro said the registration process, including filing with the police, was too cumbersome for him to keep the device, which he could not use in Beijing in any case.
He sold it to another buyer who had been looking for a Mavic but was unable to find one at a reasonable price in local stores.
More than 3.3 million drones were registered as of the end of last year in China.
Shanghai also requires owners to register their devices.

data.title: Hong Kong police investigate ‘suspicious’ fire after man dies in fall
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities are investigating a suspicious fire that broke out in a residential flat, after a man died when he fell from the building and an elderly woman was overcome due to heavy smoke.
Police received a report of the fire on Friday at around 7pm at a flat in Royal Garden, Ho Man Tin. Firefighters arrived at the scene, used a ladder to access the flat, and put out the blaze about 30 minutes later.
During the incident, a man fell from the sixth floor of the building onto a car park, while a woman in her eighties felt unwell due to heavy smoke inhalation. The pair were sent to Kwong Wah Hospital in Yau Ma Tei.
The man died after arriving at the hospital
Police said the fire was “suspicious” and they were investigating the incident.
Up to 20 residents evacuated the scene.

data.title: More Hongkongers can drive to Macau under expanded travel scheme
data.description:
More Hong Kong permanent residents will soon be able to drive to Macau for work, sightseeing, family visits and business as the two cities move to expand an existing scheme to make better use of the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge.
The Transport Department said on Friday that all Hong Kong permanent residents and locally registered companies would be allowed to apply for 400 multiple-entry quota places under a new category for drivers of non-commercial private cars.
Under the current scheme, only Hong Kong permanent residents who have paid jobs in Macau or a company registered in the city, as well as select businesses, are eligible.
Businesses applying to the scheme must be either registered in both cities or be registered in Hong Kong but associated with another business registered in Macau.
“A new open category will be introduced to make better use of the bridge’s carrying capacity and facilitate efficient cross-boundary travel,” the spokesman said.
In addition to the 400 spaces under the new category, the number of spots for Hongkongers working in Macau and select businesses will be expanded to 1,000.
The department said Hong Kong and Macau were committed to enhancing the arrangements of cross-boundary driving schemes, streamlining the application procedures and improving travel experiences.
“These new open quotas will provide additional convenience for Hong Kong residents to drive to Macau for work, business, visiting families or sightseeing, thereby strengthening ties between the two places,” it said.
Applications for the quota spots will be open from May 7 to 20. Candidates chosen by ballot will be able to drive to Macau as early as July 13. The places will remain valid until July 12, 2029.

data.title: Hong Kong taxi driver arrested for dangerous driving, hitting speeds of 160km/h
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a taxi driver for dangerous driving on the first of the five-day mainland Chinese Labour Day “golden week” holiday, after he was caught reaching speeds of 160km/h (99.4mph) and completing a 42km ride in just half an hour.
Police took enforcement action against the 31-year-old driver on Friday after intelligence officers found the arrestee, who mainly drives around Sai Kung, had frequently uploaded videos of dangerous driving online.
“Officers went undercover as tourists at Sai Kung Country Park and used an unmarked police vehicle to collect evidence,” said Inspector Pun Chun-ho of the Kowloon East enforcement and control division.
“The driver was weaving in and out of traffic on narrow and busy roads, with speeds once reaching 160km/h.”
He added the driver took about 30 minutes to complete a 42km (26 miles) ride from Sai Kung Country Park to the Heung Yuen Wai border checkpoint.
According to Google Maps, it normally takes around 40 minutes to drive 41km from the Sai Kung Country Park Visitor Centre to the Heung Yuen Wai Boundary Control Point.
In Hong Kong, the maximum permitted speed limit is 110km/h, which is only allowed on the North Lantau Highway. The highest permitted speed on most other highways is 100km/h.
Police eventually stopped the driver at the border facility and apprehended him for dangerous driving.
Pun said officers also confiscated the taxi, which was suspected of having undergone illegal modification.
Police did not reveal the suspect’s identity. But an Instagram user called “hk_taxi_dog_bb”, who claims to work as a taxi driver in Sai Kung, uploaded a selfie in front of the police Kowloon East regional headquarters. The user, who has more than 74,000 followers, later removed the photo.
Anyone convicted of dangerous driving can face a fine of up to HK$25,000 and a three-year jail term.
Separately, police also arrested two taxi drivers on Thursday, ahead of the “golden week” holiday, which runs from Friday to Tuesday on the mainland. Hong Kong authorities expect mainland visitors to make 980,000 inbound trips during the five-day break, up 7 per cent from the same period last year.
During an undercover operation in Sai Kung, the force found two taxi drivers, aged 43 and 66, overcharging more than double the original fare.
It noted that one of them charged HK$540 for a ride that initially cost HK$200, while the other asked for HK$300.

The pair were also arrested for failing to display their taxi meters and cab driver identity plates at the designated position.
The two taxis were confiscated for inspection.
Upon conviction, anyone who overcharges faces a maximum fine of HK$10,000 and six months’ imprisonment.
Police added that seven taxi drivers, who were found overcharging during the Chinese New Year holiday at the High Island Reservoir East Dam in Sai Kung, have been convicted and fined between HK$3,000 and HK$6,000. They also received 10 demerit points.
Under the system, taxi drivers will be given demerit points for 11 types of misbehaviour including overcharging and refusing fares. They could be disqualified from driving cabs if they accumulated too many points within a certain time frame.
Offences incurring the highest penalty score of 10 include overcharging, wilfully refusing or neglecting to accept hire, refusing or neglecting to drive to a location indicated by the hirer and defacing, damaging or altering the meter.

data.title: US telecoms agency votes to expand tech crackdown on China
data.description:
The Federal Communications ⁠Commission on Thursday voted unanimously to advance a proposal to bar all Chinese labs from testing electronic devices such as smartphones, cameras and computers for use in the United States.
The agency says about 75 per cent of ‌all US electronics are tested in China and plans to adopt a streamlined approval process for devices tested in US labs or labs from countries not posing national security risks.
In a separate 3-0 vote, the commission advanced a proposal to bar China Mobile, China Telecom and China Unicom from operating data centres in ⁠the US and could ban telecoms carriers from interconnecting with companies on its national security “Covered ‌List”.
Previously, the FCC barred the three companies from operating in the US.
The FCC said it was also considering barring interconnection with companies ‌that own data centres or points of presence at US internet exchange ⁠points, extending restrictions to ⁠some affiliates of listed firms, and prohibiting interconnection with carriers using equipment from suppliers on the national security ‌list including Huawei and ZTE.

FCC Chair Brendan Carr said the commission is considering a series of actions “to secure ‌our networks ‌from these bad actors”, including limiting their interconnection ability.
The moves build on previous ‌actions to clamp down on Beijing, including earlier this month a proposal to ban ⁠the import of equipment from Chinese manufacturers on the Covered List, after barring approvals of ⁠new models by those companies in 2022.
In October the FCC moved to revoke the ability of HKT, a leading Hong Kong telecoms carrier and subsidiary ‌of PCCW, to ‌operate in the US.
In December, it banned the import of all new models of Chinese drones and last month it banned imports of new models of Chinese-made consumer routers, the boxes connecting computers, phones and smart devices to the internet.

data.title: Chinese firms face pressure on AI investments as US peers’ spending soarsLargest US companies on track for US$700 billion in AI capital expenditures this year, versus about US$105 billion in China.
data.description:
Artificial intelligence investments by US tech giants continue to soar, dwarfing those of Chinese AI firms, but China’s rising appetite for AI applications will compel its tech companies to increase their AI spending this year, according to analysts.
The largest US tech companies are on track for more than US$700 billion in AI capital expenditures this year, driven in part by rising memory costs and continuously growing demand for AI applications.
Google and Microsoft on Thursday both said that their full-year capex would reach about US$190 billion, while Meta Platforms raised its capex estimates for this year to US$145 billion. Amazon kept its outlook unchanged from last year at US$200 billion.
AI spending by Chinese tech firms represented a fraction of the US level, according to analyst estimates. Chinese cloud service providers would likely spend US$105 billion this year, according to a Morgan Stanley research report last week.
Last year, the AI capex of China’s internet giants stood at 400 billion yuan (US$59 billion), about a tenth as much as their US peers, said Wei Xiong, UBS China internet industry analyst, in a report in December. Despite this “prudent” spending, Chinese firms had developed large AI models of similar calibre to those from the US, Xiong added.
“China’s AI investment scale can’t be directly compared to that of US hyperscalers as proof that it’s not investing enough in computing power, and it certainly doesn’t mean the ‘good enough’ cost-effective Chinese models are not generating comparable business returns on the market,” said Tilly Zhang, China technology and industrial policy analyst at research firm Gavekal.
Faced with restrictions on purchasing the most advanced and expensive US chips for AI training and commercial use, Chinese firms have relied more on software and algorithm improvements, she said.
Chinese tech companies’ progress in commercialising their AI platforms would drive increased spending this year, according to Morgan Stanley, which expected AI-related capex this year by China’s biggest internet platforms, including Tencent Holdings, Alibaba Group Holding, ByteDance, Baidu, Meituan and Kuaishou, to increase 38 per cent from a year earlier to 597 billion yuan.

The expansion would be supported by “clear monetisation pathways” across advertising, consumer and enterprise AI applications, the investment bank said.
Zhang added that new applications such as OpenClaw, the AI agent that triggered a frenzy in China earlier this year, had fuelled an increase in token consumption, incentivising Chinese AI firms to increase their computing power.
“Chinese companies’ AI capex plans are still much more practical, based on real commercial demand, but now it has reached a point where the market requires more capex spending,” Zhang said.
If computing power capacity was not a constraint, there would be higher than expected AI capex in China, said Chelsey Tam, Morningstar senior equity analyst.
Tencent last month pledged to double its investment in AI products and models to 18 billion yuan this year. ByteDance, operator of TikTok and its Chinese version Douyin, planned to spend about 100 billion yuan on AI chips from Nvidia this year, up from roughly 85 billion yuan in 2025, it said in December.
Alibaba in September last year announced plans to boost its AI infrastructure spending beyond the US$53 billion over three years that it pledged earlier, but it did not disclose the amount of additional investment. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

data.title: Beijing’s Xia Baolong, top energy official meet with CLP’s Michael Kadoorie
data.description:
Beijing’s point man on Hong Kong affairs and a senior national energy official have held meetings with CLP Holdings chairman Michael Kadoorie, with observers saying it signalled that the firm was expected to help minimise livelihood disruptions amid the ongoing global fuel crisis and support the Northern Metropolis megaproject.
The Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office said its director, Xia Baolong, spoke with Kadoorie, his son Philip Kadorrie and the senior management of energy firm CLP Power in Beijing on Wednesday, but stopped short of revealing any further details.
The tycoon and his team also met with He Yang, deputy director of the National Energy Administration, on Thursday.
The bureau-level organisation said the meeting focused on ensuring a secure electricity supply amid a complex international situation, as well as ensuring alignment with the energy blueprint laid out in the 15th national five-year plan covering 2026 to 2030.
It said in a statement that He highlighted mainland China’s efforts to ensure a sufficient electricity supply and stable oil and gas prices despite geopolitical tensions.
“The National Energy Administration will continue to support CLP Power in ensuring a secure and reliable electricity supply for Hong Kong,” he said.
The energy firm is a wholly-owned subsidiary of CLP Holdings and supplies electricity to Kowloon, the New Territories and Lantau Island.
The bureau said Kadoorie had reaffirmed CLP Power’s commitment to maintaining a stable power supply for Hong Kong, while proactively aligning with the latest five-year plan and taking part in major international cooperation activities the state body had organised.
A CLP spokesman declined to provide further details about the meetings.
The visit came after the firm raised its fuel cost adjustment charge in April, citing conflict in the Middle East as driving up wholesale gas and power prices.
The smaller of the city’s two electricity utilities, HK Electric, also warned of significant increases in charges later in the year due to the surge in fuel prices.
HK Electric, which is part of Li Ka-shing’s CK Hutchison Group, provides power for Hong Kong Island and Lamma Island.

Lau Siu-kai, a consultant to the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said the meetings indicated Beijing’s concern that a sharp rise in electricity costs could fuel inflation, impacting residents’ livelihoods and hampering economic development.
“[Beijing expects] utility firms not to be too aggressive when considering charge increases. They can earn less and share the hardship with Hongkongers,” Lau said, noting the central government also acknowledged the burden arising from high fuel prices.
“Beijing hopes that when raising electricity charges, the companies will adopt a prudent approach and try their best not to affect the economy and livelihood.”
Lau added that Beijing would continue to monitor the situation closely and was prepared, if necessary, to support Hong Kong’s energy supply by using the mainland’s vast fuel reserves.
Veteran political commentator Sonny Lo Shiu-hing said Beijing also hoped that CLP would step up investment in the Northern Metropolis, as technological facilities and the growing population would generate high energy consumption.
The megaproject aims to transform 30,000 hectares (74,132 acres) of land along the city’s border with the mainland into a dual engine for economic growth and housing,
Lo suggested that CLP’s meeting with the National Energy Administration potentially included discussions on the megaproject’s future energy demand and that of the Greater Bay Area as a whole, and how the firm could better coordinate with the mainland.
In an interview with the SCMP earlier this year, Kadoorie said the company would take about seven years to prepare a project in the Northern Metropolis and meet the rising energy demand generated by the initiative and a growing number of data centres.
Thursday also saw Xia meet with Jardine Matheson executive chairman Benjamin Keswick, marking the top official’s second meeting with Hong Kong business leaders in two days.
Two days earlier, Chinese Vice-Premier He Lifeng held talks with Keswick.
According to state news agency Xinhua, He said the mainland would continue to welcome foreign enterprises and encourage them to actively participate in the development of the bay area.
Lau said the back-to-back meetings underscored Beijing’s efforts to attract international investment in the face of escalating geopolitical tensions to enliven the economy.
“Lots of capital is leaving wealthier regions, including Europe, the United States and the Middle East,” Lau said.
“There is no reason why the mainland should not seize this opportunity, especially as its attractiveness to investors continues to rise, and step up efforts to draw in that capital.”

data.title: Hong Kong seizes HK$16 million of counterfeit goods in joint cross-border operation
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities uncovered 23 cross-boundary and transshipment counterfeiting cases in a joint operation with mainland China and Macau in April, seizing about 46,000 items worth an estimated HK$16 million (US$2 million).
The Customs and Excise Department said on Friday that the operation found that 21 cases involved local express couriers and logistics companies.
The remaining two cases involved batches of counterfeit goods discovered on two inbound lorries at the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge, leading to the arrest of two drivers.
The drivers, aged 48 and 52, were released on bail pending further investigation.
Customs said inspections in Hong Kong, the mainland and Macau had been stepped up to combat cross-boundary trade of counterfeit goods.
“Hong Kong customs will continue to work closely with the mainland customs, Macau customs and overseas law enforcement agencies to vigorously combat cross-boundary counterfeit goods transhipping activities through intelligence exchanges and joint enforcement actions,” it said.
The operation, carried out from April 13 to April 24, involved inspections across the three jurisdictions, with customs officers seizing suspected counterfeit goods such as watches, mobile phones, bags, clothing, footwear and electronic products.
The shipments were destined for countries in the Americas, Europe, the Middle East, Africa and Southeast Asia.
Separately, on April 23, customs raided 19 hawker stalls on Tung Choi Street in Mong Kok in an operation to crack down on the sale of counterfeit goods.
Officers seized about 1,800 suspected counterfeit items, including handbags, leather goods and accessories, with an estimated value of HK$1.1 million.
One man and three women, aged between 36 and 57, were arrested. Investigations are ongoing.
Customs said it would step up inspections and enforcement before and during the Labour Day “golden week” holiday, which for mainland residents started on Friday and runs until May 5, to safeguard consumer rights.
The department urged consumers to shop at reputable retailers and to check with trademark owners if they had doubts about a product’s authenticity. It added that traders should remain vigilant, as the sale of counterfeit goods was a “serious crime”.
In January, a joint operation between Hong Kong, mainland and Macau customs detected 14 cases of cross-boundary counterfeiting.
About 19,000 suspected counterfeit goods, including handbags, footwear and electronic products, were seized, with a total estimated market value of HK$14 million.
A 54-year-old male was arrested for transporting suspected counterfeit goods on an inbound lorry at the Hong Kong port of the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge.
Under the Trade Descriptions Ordinance, any person who imports or exports goods bearing a forged trade mark commits an offence.
The maximum penalty upon conviction is a fine of HK$500,000 and five years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Man arrested in Hong Kong after police seize HK$800,000 of suspected heroin
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a 44-year-old man for allegedly trafficking more than HK$800,000 (US$102,110) of suspected heroin.
The force said on Friday that officers from the Tuen Mun district anti-triad unit were conducting an operation at around 9.50am the day before, when they found a man acting suspiciously outside a flat on Yan Po Road.
After stopping the man for questioning, officers found about 300 grams (10.6oz) of suspected heroin in a reusable bag he was carrying. They also found an additional kilogram of heroin inside the flat.
The total market value of the drugs seized during the operation was about HK$805,000, the force said.
After a preliminary investigation, police arrested the 44-year-old Hongkonger, surnamed Chan, on suspicion of trafficking in dangerous drugs – an offence punishable by up to life in prison and a HK$5 million fine.
The man is currently being detained for investigation.
According to official statistics, serious drug offence cases climbed to 1,281 last year, up from 1,114 in 2024. The number of people arrested for drug offences also rose 27 per cent from 3,243 to 4,108 over the same period.
Meanwhile, the number of youngsters under the age of 21 arrested for such offences surged by 90 per cent from 301 to 573.
Citing court cases that concluded in 2025, the Action Committee Against Narcotics said the conviction rate of people prosecuted for drug offences was as high as 86 per cent.
Heroin, cocaine and methamphetamine were the three most common types of drugs abused in Hong Kong last year, according to Central Registry of Drug Abuse figures.
Earlier this week, the force arrested 120 people, including 14 minors, in a month-long operation targeting multiple online sales of illegal narcotics and related products.
About 53.7kg (118lbs) of dangerous drugs and Part 1 poisons were seized as part of the operation, with a market value of HK$26 million.

data.title: Hong Kong steps up enforcement as online e-cigarette sales continue despite ban
data.description:
Sales of vaping products persist on some online platforms delivering to Hong Kong despite a ban on the import, sale and public use of alternative tobacco products, prompting authorities to step up monitoring and enforcement.
The South China Morning Post on Friday found several online platforms selling e-cigarette products, with some websites displaying contact details using country codes from Malaysia and the United Kingdom.
One platform advertised delivery and after-sales service in Hong Kong, with items arriving within two days. It also carried a disclaimer stating that “due to varying laws in different regions, we strongly advise you to understand and confirm local regulations before purchasing”.
“It is your responsibility to ensure the products comply with local laws to avoid potential legal risks. If the purchased product violates local laws, we will not provide compensation or take any legal responsibility,” the notice said.
Another website registered in Malaysia stated that items were dispatched from outside Hong Kong, with delivery typically taking two working days. It also carried a similar notice advising customers to confirm that purchased products comply with local laws.
It said the company would not provide compensation or legal liabilities if the purchased product violated local laws.
Hong Kong has banned the import, manufacture, promotion and sale of alternative smoking products, including e-cigarettes, since 2022, and extended the prohibition to their use in public from Thursday.
Dr Manny Lam Man-chung, head of the Tobacco and Alcohol Control Office, said on Friday that authorities had enforced the law rigorously and intensified online monitoring of violations.

“The ban on the sale and import of alternative smoking products has been in effect for four years. We attach great importance to enforcement and violations after the law was implemented,” he said.
“We have employed a variety of measures, including investigating and following up on complaints and intelligence reports, proactively strengthening patrols and online monitoring, and enhancing intelligence sharing and joint operations between different law enforcement agencies.
“If there are any illegal cases, we will prosecute them to increase deterrence.”
Under the new ban, anyone found carrying more than five vape pods or 100 heated tobacco sticks in public faces a maximum fine of HK$50,000 (US$6,400) and up to six months in prison. Those carrying smaller quantities face a fixed penalty of HK$3,000.
Lam said authorities would enhance patrols, enforcement and publicity over the next two weeks to raise public awareness of the new ban, particularly in high-traffic areas.
Authorities conducted 137 inspections by 5pm on Thursday, issuing 12 fixed-penalty notices for smoking offences and four under new legislation targeting alternative tobacco products.
Despite the ban, the SCMP observed some Hong Kong residents continuing to vape in secluded corners and back alleys in busy districts such as Wan Chai and Causeway Bay.
In previous cases, police have uncovered etomidate packaged as e-cigarette capsules.
Health authorities previously said the ban would help prevent more young people from taking up e-cigarettes, which they found more appealing than traditional cigarettes.
Hong Kong’s overall smoking rate among those aged 15 and above was 8.5 per cent in 2025, while daily vape use was below 1 per cent in 2023.
In the 2022–23 academic year, 1.1 per cent of secondary students and 0.5 per cent of Primary Four to Six pupils used e-cigarettes.

data.title: Hong Kong’s East Dam draws thousands for ‘golden week’ as entry fee floated
data.description:
Thousands of visitors have flocked to Hong Kong’s East Dam to view its rare volcanic rock columns at the start of the five-day Labour Day “golden week” holiday, prompting a lawmaker to suggest charging an entrance fee to maintain the Unesco global geopark.
Drawn by the city’s natural scenery and well-established hiking trails heavily promoted on mainland Chinese social media, many tourists are increasingly trading traditional shopping sprees for ecotourism at East Dam of the High Island Reservoir in Sai Kung on Friday.
By 2pm, authorities reported that visitor traffic at the East Dam had already reached the “very busy” level. Citywide, the government anticipates about 980,000 mainland visitors during the holiday period, a 7 per cent year-on-year increase.
The East Dam attracted a diverse crowd of mainland tourists, foreigners and local residents – including domestic helpers – who arrived in groups to rest and dine around the pavilions.
Visitors showed they were mindful of environmental protection, and many praised police and government officers for maintaining order. Many also found the mobile network acceptable and came prepared with their own supplies, opting to wander leisurely without setting shopping budgets.
Zoe Jiang, a 32-year-old medical worker from Shenzhen, visited with two friends from Chengdu after discovering the site on the mainland social media platform Xiaohongshu, or RedNote.
“It’s good for photo-taking, and it’s beautiful. It’s energetic and healing being close to nature,” Jiang said. “The landscape is different. Hiking here is more beautiful. Shenzhen people like to hike in Hong Kong. Although the cost is higher, we like the natural scenery here.”
Jiang planned a day trip, while her Chengdu friends will visit Hong Kong Disneyland and other places in the coming days.
Qin Xiang, 25, a new media worker from Shenzhen, was similarly drawn by the online buzz. Hiking the renowned MacLehose Trail, he noted a surge in outdoor activities.
Hong Kong’s hiking trails “developed early with more mature routes than the mainland,” Qin said, adding that the site was noticeably more crowded than his last visit in 2024.
“This appears to be the top view around Shenzhen. I will go back after walking the second section … via Sai Wan.”

For others, the geopark offered an escape. Designer Liu Jinzhong, 27, said he travelled from Foshan for overnight camping to relieve himself from the “tedious” city work, while Xingjun, a 43-year-old merchant from Changsha, joined an all-inclusive two-day camping tour with her husband and teenage son so the family could travel light.
International visitors also braved the crowds. Edgar Hahn, a German energy sector professional in his thirties living in Tokyo, visited on a friend’s recommendation.
“I’m actually surprised that it’s that crowded,” Hahn said, but he commended the government’s efforts.
“They put quite a bit of effort into sorting the garbage, and there are a lot of transportable toilets.”
Police officers and government officials “were very smiling” and “when you greet them, they would greet back”, Hahn added.
Despite the positive atmosphere, infrastructure limitations remain a hurdle. The reporter’s taxi ride was smooth until reaching the West Dam, where a 20-minute bottleneck ensued.

Dozens of exiting taxis and minibuses had to navigate a narrow, single-lane road shared with hikers and wandering cows, before a new line of incoming cars could enter.
Taxi driver Lo Chi-keung expressed confusion over the holiday traffic arrangements, noting he was completely barred from driving to the East Dam during the Mid-Autumn Festival last year.
At the East Dam, visitors faced queues even at the men’s toilets at one time. Meanwhile, police, Tourism Authority staff, a fire engine, and Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department (AFCD) officers stood guard.
To manage the crowds and enhance safety, the AFCD had recently installed additional railings at the Po Pin Chau viewing platform.
Lawmaker and Sai Kung district councillor Christine Fong Kwok-shan, who regularly monitored the site during major holidays, suggested charging an entrance fee of HK$80 (US$10.20) to HK$100 for the Unesco global geopark – similar to practices at mainland attractions.
“I hope to help Hong Kong generate money while offering a good travel experience,” Fong said. “The revenue can specifically be for maintenance, tourists and hygiene.”
Fong suggested setting up a basic water point at the West Dam rather than installing commercial vending machines or shops.
She warned that sustaining high footfall poses a significant challenge to the park, as the area is fundamentally a nature reserve and lacks major infrastructure such as running water, electricity and sewage pipes.
“We are building a washroom at Long Ke Wan,” Fong said. “But if there are too many people, this will be a big problem. This is nature; we are not going to develop this place.”

data.title: Samsung, SK Hynix flag record supply squeeze in memory market as AI demand soars
data.description:
Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, South Korea’s two memory chip giants, are warning of a prolonged and severe global supply crunch, weeks after the two companies disclosed increased investments in their China wafer fabs to meet surging artificial intelligence demand.
During Samsung’s first-quarter earnings call on Thursday, the company said its order fulfilment rate had plunged to a “record low”. In a rare move, customers worried about shortages were already pre-booking memory capacity for 2027, according to company executives.
Available supply was far short of customer demand, executives said, adding that production-ready capacity for the next-generation high bandwidth memory – the HBM4 – was fully booked.
The two South Korean chipmakers remain the largest beneficiaries of the memory supercycle. Explosive AI demand, especially from the rapid adoption of AI agents, has pushed Samsung’s earnings to record levels. Quarterly revenue jumped 69 per cent year on year to 133.9 trillion won (US$90.25 billion), while operating profit surged nearly eightfold to 57.2 trillion won.
Samsung’s warning of supply-demand imbalance echoes similar concerns from rival SK Hynix, which released its own record-breaking first-quarter results last week. Revenue soared nearly 200 per cent year on year to 52.6 trillion won, driven by structural demand for advanced memory chips.

Demand from major customers was increasing across the board – including HBM, dynamic random access memory and enterprise solid-state drives – while suppliers struggled to increase output, said Kim Woo-hyun, chief financial officer of SK Hynix, at the earnings call. He added that the current rising price cycle was expected to last longer than past industry cycles.
The tight supply situation and favourable pricing environment were expected to persist, with strong conventional memory prices feeding into future HBM pricing, Goldman Sachs said in a research note on Wednesday.
To address supply constraints, Samsung and SK Hynix are drastically increasing capital expenditure, anticipating substantial year-on-year increases in 2026. China, notably, is playing an increasingly important role amid their production expansion plans. The earnings call came after the companies in March disclosed moves to increase investments in their China wafer fabs last year.
Samsung invested 465.4 billion won in its Xian chip plant in 2025, up 67.5 per cent from a year earlier, according to its filing in mid-March. SK Hynix also ramped up spending to 581.1 billion won and 440.6 billion won in its facilities in Wuxi and Dalian, respectively.
Samsung, meanwhile, is reportedly considering a broad restructuring of its China business, sharpening focus on semiconductor and smartphone units while potentially exiting segments such as home appliances and displays, according to Chinese media outlet Yicai in early April, citing anonymous industry insiders.
Beyond South Korea, other memory chipmakers in China and the US also saw revenue surge amid the supercycle, according to their earnings releases on Wednesday.
China’s GigaDevice, the flash memory firm founded by CXMT co-founder Zhu Yiming, saw its first-quarter revenue and profit jump 119 per cent and 523 per cent year on year, respectively, while US data storage company Seagate Technology reported a quarterly revenue of US$3.11 billion, beating guidance.

data.title: Hong Kong homebuyers flock to new launches as flats sell out swiftly
data.description:
Hong Kong homebuyers snapped up most of the new flats released on Thursday, with developers beginning to ramp up new construction amid sustained housing demand.
All 120 units at the One Victoria Cove I in Hung Hom, jointly developed by Henderson Land Development, Hysan Development and Empire Group, were sold by 5.20pm, according to agents. Meanwhile, New World Development (NWD) and MTR Corp’s Pavilia Farm III above Tai Wai MTR station found buyers for 19 of the 45 units as of 6.50pm.
Most recent flats offerings have seen robust sales. Earlier this month, the first two batches of Pavilia Farm III, and the previous round of One Victoria Cove I sold out. This followed a full take-up of flats at La Mirabelle I in Tseung Kwan O by Sino Land, Kerry Properties, K. Wah International, China Merchants Land and MTR Corp in late March.
NWD said in a statement on Thursday that it had generated more than HK$4.8 billion (US$610 million) from the sale of 295 units at Pavilia Farm III in a span of 11 days.

“Sales of new homes in the first four months of 2026 reached about 8,500 units, of which around 3,000 flats came from completed projects, mainly in areas such as Kai Tak, Tseung Kwan O and Sai Sha,” said Norry Lee, senior director for projects strategy and consultancy at JLL.
The sales reduced developers’ unsold inventory to below 20,000, back to 2023 levels, Lee said.
As a result, developers have started building more new flats. The number of private housing units under construction rose for two consecutive quarters to 4,100 at the end of the first quarter this year, up from 800 in the third quarter of 2025, according to data from the Housing Bureau.
“As recent sales of new launches remain strong, the potential supply of new flats over the next three to four years is expected to fall below 100,000 units in the second half of the year,” Lee said.
As developers made limited land acquisitions over the past three years, only 1,600 new private flats were completed in the first quarter of the year.
“Developers have turned active in the land market since early this year,” Lee said. “We expect the completion of new flats will return to a healthy level in 2028.”
The bureau estimated that 101,000 new private units would be available in the coming three to four years, lower than the estimated 104,000 units at the end of 2025.
“The reduced inventory in the past quarter was primarily due to the property market recovery and developers’ strategy of offering units at market prices,” said Derek Chan Hoi-chiu, head of research at Ricacorp Properties. “The reduced pressure from unsold units has significantly lessened developers’ urgency to undercut prices, positively impacting the overall price increase.”
At One Victoria Cove I on Thursday, units on sale ranged from 301 sq ft to 642 sq ft with two to three-bedroom layouts. They were sold at discounted prices of between HK$6.85 million and HK$14 million.
“A significant proportion of buyers [at One Victoria Cove I] are investors, reflecting strong interest in projects offering small flats,” Lee said.
“Such large developments are more attractive to tenants. Also, the project is located near the Hong Kong Polytechnic University and Hong Kong Metropolitan University, where leasing demand from non-local students continues to grow.”
The latest batch of flats at Pavilia Farm III consisted of one and two-bedroom units ranging from 359 sq ft to 509 sq ft and priced from HK$9.22 million to HK$15.37 million.

data.title: Hongkongers flock to Shenzhen for ‘better’ Labour Day dining and attractions
data.description:
Hong Kong residents flocked to Shenzhen on Friday, drawn by ‘better service’ at restaurants and family-friendly attractions as the Labour Day “golden week” holiday began.
Affordability has also lured droves of Hongkongers across the border, with families budgeting about HK$1,000 (US$128) for a full day in the tech hub, covering restaurants, dental clinics, indoor amusement parks and ice-skating rinks.
Labour Day is a statutory public holiday in Hong Kong and marks the start of mainland China’s five-day holiday from May 1 to 5.
While about 980,000 mainland visitors are expected to enter Hong Kong via sea, land and air control points during the holiday, scores of residents packed trains bound for Shenzhen at Lok Wu and Lok Ma Chau stations.
At 10.30am, residents streamed through immigration checkpoints at Lok Ma Chau station, with queues clearing within minutes. By about 11am, Lo Wu station was packed as arrivals came in waves, many hurrying to beat the crowds and cross the border.
Financial worker Steven Kwan arrived at Lok Ma Chau station at about 9.40am with his wife, three children and mother for a day trip to Shenzhen.
The 43-year-old said the family was heading to Link Central Walk, a shopping centre in the heart of Futian district, where they planned to visit Fundoland, an indoor arcade with a range of games including claw machines and racing simulators.
Kwan said the family had set aside around HK$1,000 to sample food at the mall.
“It (Shenzhen) is bigger, with newer offerings and better restaurant service,” he said. “[In some eateries] each table is exclusively served by one or two waiters.”
Clerk Cherry Chan was heading to Shenzhen with her husband and two children for a day trip. Her priority was to have a dental check-up and explore more affordable implant options.
“In Hong Kong it can cost HK$18,000 for a dental implant, in Shenzhen, a centre charges HK$3,000 to HK$6,000,” she said.

The family will then head to Wongtee Plaza, also in the Futian district, known for its wide range of eateries, from hotpot to baked goods and desserts. She noted that restaurants there were generally less crowded and did not impose dining time limits, unlike those in Hong Kong.
“Visiting Shenzhen is like visiting the outlying islands in Hong Kong to us,” she said. “We will spend locally in Hong Kong during the rest of the Golden Week holiday too.”
As of 10am, the city recorded 133,490 outbound trips, of which 110,007, or 82 per cent, were made by Hongkongers, according to Immigration Department figures.
More than 80 per cent of these outbound trips — about 90,000 — were made via control points linking the city with the mainland.
At Lo Wu station at around 10.15am, 40-year-old Rick Lee said he would take his wife and two young children to Shenzhen’s Window of the World theme park, where they planned to use the indoor ice rink at its winter sports section, Alpine Ice and Snow World.
Lee, who also budgeted around HK$1,000 for his family’s one-night stay and spending, said he chose the mainland city to avoid crowds.
“Hong Kong was always very crowded, and family-friendly options were typically only Ocean Park or Disneyland during the holidays,” he said.
A man, who only gave his first name as Don, said he would visit Shenzhen’s Three Express Trains Museum, located within a trendy cultural park.
The man, who regularly travels between Hong Kong and the Pearl River Delta, said he will also take his son for sushi in Shenzhen, describing it as better than in Hong Kong but trailing behind Shanghai.
He said Hong Kong restaurants were more expensive due to infrastructure costs and rent, making them less competitive.
“The restaurants [in Hong Kong] are also too conservative. The product lines are too limited,” he said.

data.title: ‘Never knew there were corals,’ visitors say on Sharp Island’s reefs
data.description:
Mainland Chinese tourists may have come for the tombolo on Sharp Island in Sai Kung but left with a deeper understanding of Hong Kong’s coral reefs and marine life after the government stepped up promotion and protection efforts.
That was the view of visitors touring the island on Friday morning, Labour Day – a Hong Kong public holiday and the start of the mainland’s five-day “golden week” break.
As of noon, around 300 tourists had arrived on the island, but only a few had entered the water.
The tombolo, a narrow sandbar connecting the island’s two landmasses, was packed with tourists.
“We never knew there were corals near the coast,” said Gloria Fu, 30, an equity trader from the mainland who works in Hong Kong, adding that the global conservation body WWF’s booth was the only coral-related promotion she saw on her way from Hap Mun Bay to the island.

Fu said she found WWF’s introduction useful for learning more about the island’s marine life.
Similarly, another tourist, Shen Siyuan from Shanghai, said she was unaware of other promotions at the pier and felt that the WWF booth on the island, with detailed introductions, was more effective.
Besides the booth, the government had set up banners near the tombolo and the pier. Many officers handed out guidelines and brochures to tourists and reminded them to take their rubbish away.
Xia Lijun, 47, from Anhui province, said she had seen the island on WeChat reels and did not know there were many corals there.
“We have prepared some bags to take our rubbish away after seeing some tips on social media, but we just learned about the corals after seeing the promotions here,” she said.
The Immigration Department estimates that around 980,000 mainland visitors will enter Hong Kong by sea, land and air from May 1 to 5, or 7 per cent more than a year ago.
Sharp Island is not part of a marine park or reserve in Hong Kong so the coral reefs, seahorses and other marine life along its coastline have no statutory protection.


It is a constraint government officials have been working around rather than through.
The Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department said on Monday that it would deploy 11 officers to the island daily from around 9am to 6pm during the holiday, with drones conducting hourly surveillance over the beach and coastal areas.
Volunteers from environmental group WWF-Hong Kong will staff a public education booth near the beach, while snorkelling guides will patrol nearshore waters to steer visitors away from coral-rich zones.
The AFCD also collaborated with the Environmental Hygiene Department, the Marine Department and police to strengthen the cleaning of public areas and maintain order.
The department estimated the island would receive up to 1,000 visitors daily during the holiday.

Local residents also welcomed the government’s action.
“The banners are effective in promoting the protection of local marine life,” said Cheung Chun-shing, a retired Sai Kung resident.
“We welcome tourists to come, but they have to keep the area clean and not take anything away.”
Cat Sun, 38, a housewife who had just surfaced from snorkelling, said the abundance of coral beneath the surface surprised her.
“I’m not worried about visitors damaging the ecosystem this time,” she said. “I’ve seen many outreach by environmental protection organisations on the island, and they came here themselves because they care about the ocean.”

data.title: Hong Kong a key global hub for USDC cross-border payments: Circle CEO
data.description:
Stablecoin payments into Hong Kong are surging on the cross-border payment network of Circle Internet Group, operator of the world’s second largest stablecoin issuer USDC, as the US firm eyes regulatory approval in the Asian crypto hub, its CEO said in an interview.
“Hong Kong remains a central trade hub, and cross-border payments and cross-border trade are major drivers in the adoption of stablecoins,” said Jeremy Allaire, co-founder and CEO of Circle.
He said that payments into Hong Kong were “one of the most popular routes for cross-border settlements”, based on data from the Circle Payments Network (CPN), a blockchain-based platform launched last May that primarily facilitates institutional settlement via stablecoins.
Driven by this high level of activity, Allaire described Hong Kong as an “important and attractive” market for Circle. “We’ve been very constructive about the emerging stablecoin regime here,” said Allaire, adding that the company hoped to see USDC approved as a foreign stablecoin in the city.
Allaire, however, said Circle did not intend to issue a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin.

Circle has already laid the groundwork for USDC. In late April, it announced a partnership with Hong Kong-listed OSL Group, a digital asset trading platform, to expand USDC offerings on OSL’s platforms. In 2024, the company signed a memorandum of understanding with HKT to explore blockchain-based customer loyalty solutions for merchants in Hong Kong.
HKT is a backer of Anchorpoint Financial, a Standard Chartered-led joint venture with participation from digital asset firm Animoca. Along with HSBC, Anchorpoint was one of the first two entities to receive a stablecoin issuer licence in Hong Kong.
These inaugural approvals are likely just the beginning. The city’s Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po noted at February’s Consensus conference that more stablecoin licences were to come.
With several issuers expected to enter the space, Allaire anticipated that Hong Kong’s competitive landscape would mirror that of the US. It would likely become a “winner-take-most” market dominated by two or three players, he added.
Allaire said a successful stablecoin needed to “seamlessly convert and move value” between the domestic and international financial systems, maintain liquidity against major US-pegged stablecoins like USDC, and establish local use cases and distribution.
The race is already on between Hong Kong’s newly licenced players. HSBC plans to integrate stablecoins into its consumer app PayMe, while Anchorpoint Financial’s HKDAP stablecoin will initially target institutional users for cross-border payments and tokenised asset settlement.
For sufficient stablecoin liquidity, Hong Kong needs a diverse range of participants across both exchange and over-the-counter markets, along with deeper integration into stablecoin FX markets and global payment networks, according to Allaire.

However, achieving this level of participation may take time. Despite the enthusiasm about blockchain, Allaire believed that most traditional finance companies were still in the nascent stage.
Within different markets around the world, Allaire pointed to a “direct correlation” that he had observed: markets with more “clarity of law” tended to exhibit acceleration in mainstream, institutional adoption, with Indonesia as an example.
In addition to legal clarity, Allaire said the proliferation of AI agents was another major driver for stablecoin use. This evolution would require a parallel shift in oversight, with regulators potentially establishing “Know Your Agent” rules to standardise how the identity and capabilities of agents were tracked and verified.

data.title: Hikes, city walks draw 76,056 mainland visitors as ‘golden week’ begins
data.description:
Hong Kong recorded more than 76,000 mainland Chinese visitor arrivals on the first morning of the five-day Labour Day “golden week”, with many heading to the countryside for scenic views and others opting for “city walks” to explore picturesque corners of the city.
Some early arrivals headed straight to the countryside after getting off at West Kowloon rail terminus on Friday morning.
University student Chen Hongjie, 18, was all geared up to hike the MacLehose Trail on his first day in the city after a three-hour train journey from Changsha.
Planning to camp along part of the trail for two nights with a friend, Chen packed sleeping mats, instant food and downloaded maps on his phone.
“I had done some research on the trip and gathered tips from travellers who had been to the same place,” he said, referring to travel content he saved on Chinese social media that recommended ways to explore Hong Kong’s nature more deeply.
Chen said he would stay one more night in the city centre after the hike, hoping to experience its vibrant urban life.

Immigration statistics showed that a total of 253,059 cross-border trips were recorded as of 10am, including 87,429 inbound visitors.
Of these, 76,056 were from mainland China, while non-mainland visitors accounted for 11,193.
Meanwhile, the city recorded a total of 133,490 outbound trips, of which 110,007, or 82 per cent, were made by Hongkongers, according to Immigration Department figures.
More than 80 per cent of these trips, or about 90,000, were made through border control points connecting the city with the mainland.
The airport was the busiest control point in the morning, with 43,140 cross-border trips. Lok Ma Chau Spur Line, Lo Wu and Shenzhen Bay recorded 35,315, 33,965 and 32,042 trips, respectively.
The High Island Reservoir’s East Dam in Sai Kung was packed with thousands of tourists, many from the mainland, drawn by its natural scenery. The site has become a hotspot among mainland visitors after being promoted on various social media platforms.
Unlike during the Chinese New Year holiday in February, when mainland visitors enjoyed a nine-day break, the terminus was less crowded this time, with fewer travellers carrying large suitcases.
Groups of relatively young visitors travelled light, planning day trips before heading back in the evening.
A 17-year-old high school student, who gave her name only as Emily, came to Hong Kong with three classmates from Guangzhou.
The four girls said they were familiar with popular tips for visiting Hong Kong and were eager to try famous caramel custard tarts in Tsim Sha Tsui, as well as puffs and other pastries in Central and Causeway Bay.
In the heart of the city, a former police station in Kowloon that has been converted into a heritage landmark welcomed hundreds of mainland visitors, as families and students flocked to the site for immersive “citywalk” experiences and short-haul itineraries.
Chen Jie, an electronics salesman from Guangxi, arrived at West Kowloon rail terminus with his son on the first day of their trip and headed straight to the historic site to take photos with movie posters, including those for the film Infernal Affairs.
“We could not get tickets, so we just took some photos outside as a memory,” he said.
The family plans to stay for two days with an estimated total budget of HK$4,000 (US$510) and intends to visit Kennedy Town and Victoria Harbour to enjoy the night views.

The Old Yau Ma Tei Police Station, a Grade II historic building and one of Kowloon’s oldest police structures, has remained a popular destination for tourists and film enthusiasts since it ceased routine operations in 2016.
The government launched a permanent ground-floor exhibition titled “Yau Ma Tei Police Station: A Cinematic Journey” on January 2, featuring recreated film sets such as an interrogation room and detention cells.
Tickets for the exhibition over the next three days have sold out, with some visitors securing their spots well in advance.
Among them is Kevin Wang, a university student from Guangzhou who arrived with his friends on Thursday.
“We saw on RedNote that the exhibition will be giving away souvenirs during the holiday, so we snapped up tickets online,” he said. “Some of us grew up watching TVB dramas, and we wanted to take photos of the crime and police settings inside.”
The group said they would leave later in the day due to a limited budget, but planned to visit landmarks such as Tsim Sha Tsui before departing.
While the nearby Jordan area also offers attractions such as the Temple Street Night Market and the Jade Market, many tourists appeared to be unaware of them.

Liu, an aviation worker from Shenzhen who was visiting while transiting through Hong Kong during the Labour Day holiday, said she had no fixed itinerary.
“I saw road signs for the street markets on my way, but I did not know they were tourist attractions,” she said.
Meanwhile, at Tai Kwun, a revitalised police station in Central that now serves as a hub for arts, culture and dining, dozens of mainland visitors were seen taking photos and soaking in the atmosphere.
A 26-year-old visitor from Shenzhen, Zhang Yuting, said she came mainly to take photos after seeing recommendations on social media, but had little intention of spending much.
“I saw a lot of people posting pictures here, so I wanted to come and take some myself,” she said. “The buildings and the atmosphere are quite nice.”
But she added that dining options in the area were beyond her budget.
“The restaurants are a bit too expensive for me. I’ll just take photos here and then go somewhere cheaper to eat,” she said.

City guides on RedNote have widely recommended the location for cross-border visitors, as tourists increasingly seek more in-depth, culturally focused itineraries rather than traditional hotspots.
A couple from Guangzhou, surnamed Chen and Huang, said they were visiting Hong Kong for two days over the Labour Day holiday and had prioritised attractions recommended on social media over shopping malls.
“We saw a lot of posts about Tai Kwun and the nearby streets, so we came to check it out,” Chen said. “It feels more unique than just going to shopping centres.”
Huang added that while prices in Hong Kong were “still quite high”, they were willing to spend on experiences.
“We might buy something small, but mainly it’s for the atmosphere.”

data.title: Gold, cruises and Dior: Hong Kong lenders aim to entice mainland China visitors
data.description:
Gold bars, cruises, Dior beauty services and Rosewood Hong Kong vouchers are just some of the incentives Hong Kong’s major lenders are offering in hopes of capturing wealthy clients, as visitors from mainland China descend on the city during the golden week holiday starting on Friday.
HSBC, Standard Chartered and Bank of China (Hong Kong) – the city’s three biggest banks – Citigroup, DBS Hong Kong, ICBC Asia, Bank of East Asia and Hang Seng Bank have all extended the hours of certain branches on Friday and through the weekend to sign up new customers, offering them cash awards and other incentives.
The five-day holiday that runs until Tuesday is a prime opportunity to sell investments, insurance and other products that fall under the wealth-management category, a major growth engine for the banks.
The Immigration Department expects about 980,000 mainland visitors to enter Hong Kong between Friday and Tuesday, an increase of 7 per cent over the same period last year. The number of entries and exits during the five-day period is expected to reach 6 million.
“Golden week is a rare window when many mainland customers are in Hong Kong at the same time,” said Frankie Yan, head of retail and wealth distribution, retail banking and wealth at HSBC Hong Kong. “Instead of just a holiday excursion, we know they want to get personal chores done – including banking.”

HSBC Life, the city’s biggest life insurance company in terms of new sales, has teamed up with Dior Beauty to offer personal beauty services at HSBC’s wealth centre, and is offering a horse racing lunch at Sha Tin Racecourse on Sunday.
The lender is also offering an HK$18,000 (US$2,307) Royal Caribbean cruise suite to customers who invest at least HK$20 million and meet certain other requirements, in addition to a standard HK$88,000 welcome offer.
Standard Chartered is holding a lucky draw for new cross-border private banking or priority banking clients, with a Rosewood hotel stay worth more than HK$10,000 as the top prize, on top of a cash rebate of HK$68,888 if they can meet certain investment criteria.
Eliza Law, head of the affluent segment and distribution at Standard Chartered Hong Kong, said offering both wealth-management advice and a range of entertainment options during golden week had proved useful to expand its business.
“Demand for cross-boundary wealth-management services remains strong,” Law said. “In May last year, the number of new priority private and priority banking clients recorded double-digit year-on-year growth, reflecting strong demand for wealth-management services among visitors to Hong Kong.”
BOCHK has focused on the rising price of gold as a selling point, offering a lucky draw for cross-border customers with the top prize of a 50g gold bar, priced at around HK$58,000 based on the gold price at the end of April.
The lender recorded a 30 per cent increase in sign-ups by wealthy cross-border customers during golden week last year, compared with normal weeks.
Golden week is a period when customer footfall in key districts typically increases, owing to overlapping public holidays in Hong Kong and the mainland, said Maggie Yung, head of DBS Treasures and distribution at DBS Hong Kong.
“For DBS, it is an opportunity to engage walk‑in visitors, maintain service accessibility and reinforce our presence in high‑traffic locations,” she said. New customers who open a new DBS Treasures account at select branches during golden week can get top-up cash awards.
Citibank is offering HK$170,000 in cash to new private banking customers and HK$70,000 to Citigold customers, provided they join the city’s investment migration scheme and meet certain investment criteria.
Greater Bay Area customers who sign up to use the Wealth Management Connect service through Citibank and invest 3 million yuan (US$438,708) will get a cash rebate of HK$13,000.

data.title: Experts warn of lasting harm after alleged dog cage child abuse case
data.description:
Severe abuse can leave lasting mental scars and behavioural problems in children, psychologists have warned after two toddlers in Hong Kong were allegedly confined in a dog cage, a case that sparked public outrage and renewed calls for earlier reporting and intervention.
The calls for action came as the latest official statistics showed that more than 100 reports had been made since a mandatory reporting requirement for suspected child abuse came into effect in January.
Professor Hou Wai-kai, from the department of psychology at the Education University of Hong Kong, said adverse childhood experiences – traumatic events affecting youngsters, such as violence and abuse – could have long-term negative impacts on health and well-being.
He said these experiences could lead to mental health conditions such as anxiety and depression, as well as behavioural problems including inattention, impulsivity, conduct disorder, and oppositional defiant disorder, which is characterised by a persistent pattern of anger and irritability.
“Many mental disorders are related to adverse childhood experiences, which can affect one’s physical and mental health during childhood, adolescence and adulthood,” he said.
The abuse of children has once again been thrust into the spotlight after a domestic helper was arrested for allegedly confining two toddlers in her care in a dog cage.

Police said they received a report on Tuesday from the boys’ mother, who alleged that the helper had placed the children, aged two and three, in a cage at a flat in the Grand Horizon estate in Tsing Yi on two occasions in February.
A police source said the mother discovered the incidents twice through her mobile phone’s security camera feed.
However, she did not report the matter until Tuesday, when she informed the employment agency and later contacted police, saying she remained silent earlier for fear of harm to her children, according to the source.
Police arrested the 32-year-old Indonesian helper on suspicion of ill-treatment.
Hou, who is director of the university’s Centre for Psychosocial Health, said such abuse could have lasting impacts on youngsters, urging parents to promptly report suspected mistreatment to authorities for early assessment and intervention.
Children who had suffered abuse should be assessed for potential impacts, while parents and teachers should watch for signs of abnormal behaviour, he said.
“Large-scale data from different countries show that the more complex a child’s mental health issues are, the higher the likelihood of developing behavioural or psychological problems in adulthood,” he said.
Paul Wong Wai-ching, a clinical psychologist and associate professor at the University of Hong Kong’s department of social work and social administration, said minors who had experienced trauma should be closely monitored, including by trusted professionals such as counsellors, psychologists and social workers.
He also renewed calls for professionals working with children to report abuse without delay.
Hong Kong has stepped up efforts to strengthen child protection, with one of the latest measures being the Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse Ordinance, which took effect on January 20.
The law requires 25 categories of professionals in the social welfare, education and healthcare sectors to report suspected serious child abuse to authorities.
Under the ordinance, those who fail to do so face up to three months in prison and a fine of HK$50,000 (US$6,400).
The latest statistics from the Social Welfare Department showed that authorities had received 102 reports under the regime as of the end of March, involving 107 children.
Of the reports, 65 were made by professionals in the social welfare sector, 26 by those in the healthcare sector and 11 by those in the education sector.
“Mandatory reporting is important. Professionals should report suspected abuse as soon as possible to avoid delays in help-seeking,” Wong said.
Sze Lai-shan, deputy director of the Society for Community Organisation, said that in addition to installing surveillance cameras at home, providing childcare training for domestic workers could help prevent abuse by raising their awareness of child protection. She added that authorities should take the lead in offering such courses.
She also noted that many parents hired domestic helpers due to a shortage of childcare services and called for more services to be provided at centres and in schools.
Parents should also provide a supportive and flexible home environment for their helpers, including giving them breaks when needed or when they were occupied with other household chores, she said.
“Even one case of child abuse is too many,” she said.
